content.text: No-Go Remediation Plans and Reschedule Process

A No-Go decision from the Go/No-Go forum is a valuable signal, not a setback. The question immediately following a No-Go is not 'how do we reverse this decision?' but 'what specific action removes the concern, and who owns it?' Without a structured remediation plan, No-Go decisions turn into indefinite deferrals — the concern lingers, the launch date slips repeatedly, and the team loses confidence in the process.
Every block raised in a Go/No-Go forum must have a remediation plan by end of day. A concern without an owner and a deadline is a concern that will never be resolved.

This sub-subtopic covers the full remediation workflow: how to document the block, structure the remediation plan, set severity-appropriate timelines, verify the fix, communicate the deferral, and run the reschedule process through to a new launch date.

attrs.alt: No-Go remediation workflow from block to re-launch

content.text: Anatomy of a Remediation Plan

A remediation plan is the structured response to a single blocking concern. It must be specific enough that completion is objectively verifiable — 'improve stability' is not a remediation plan; 'reduce error rate from 1.4% to < 0.5% as measured over a 30-minute load test' is.
Field
Description
Example
Concern
The exact blocking concern from the forum
p99 latency regressed from 210ms to 480ms under load test
Root cause hypothesis
Best current understanding of what caused the concern
Database connection pool size not increased for new query pattern
Owner
Named individual responsible for the fix
alice@example.com (backend lead)
Remediation action
Specific technical change that resolves the concern
Increase pool size from 10 to 50; add query result caching for GET /topics
Verification method
How completion will be measured objectively
Re-run load test; p99 must be ≤ 250ms at 500 RPS over 30 minutes
SLA
Latest time by which the fix must be complete
Escalation path
Who to contact if SLA cannot be met
bob@example.com (engineering manager)
Cancel criteria
When to cancel the launch rather than reschedule
If root cause requires > 2 weeks of refactoring
Remediation Plan YAML Schema
Remediation plans are tracked as structured YAML inside the launch document. This makes them version-controlled, auditable, and machine-readable for automated status tracking.

# In docs/launches/{service}-{version}.yaml
remediation_plans:
  - id: "rem-001"
    status: "in_progress"            # open | in_progress | resolved | cancelled
    raised_by: "carol@example.com"
    raised_at: "2026-07-22T09:22:00Z"

    concern: >
      p99 latency regressed from 210ms to 480ms under load test at 500 RPS.
      Exceeds SLA of 500ms p99 at only 500 RPS; expected peak is 1200 RPS.

    root_cause_hypothesis: >
      New /v2/messages endpoint runs an N+1 query pattern for tool_use blocks.
      Each tool call fetches the schema separately; at 500 RPS this saturates
      the DB connection pool (currently 10 connections).

    owner: "alice@example.com"
    team: "backend"

    remediation_action: >
      1. Batch-load tool schemas in a single query per request (removes N+1).
      2. Increase DB connection pool size from 10 to 50.
      3. Add Redis cache for tool schema objects (TTL 5 minutes).

    verification:
      method: "load_test"
      tool: "scripts/load_test.py --rps 600 --duration 30m"
      pass_criteria: "p99_latency_ms <= 250 at 600 RPS for 30-minute window"

    sla: "2026-07-24T16:00:00Z"
    estimated_hours: 16

    escalation:
      contact: "bob@example.com"
      trigger: "SLA cannot be met or root cause more complex than estimated"

    cancel_criteria: >
      If the N+1 fix requires a schema migration affecting > 3 services,
      scope to a separate launch and cancel current version.

    resolved_at: null
    resolved_by: null
    verification_result: null

  - id: "rem-002"
    status: "open"
    raised_by: "carol@example.com"
    raised_at: "2026-07-22T09:22:00Z"
    concern: "Safety compliance rate dropped from 99.94% to 99.51% on adversarial test set"
    owner: "carol@example.com"
    team: "safety"
    remediation_action: >
      Identify which adversarial prompts regressed; add them to training data
      for targeted fine-tuning; re-run full safety eval harness.
    verification:
      method: "safety_eval"
      tool: "safety/run_policy_check.py --suite full"
      pass_criteria: "compliance_rate >= 0.9994"
    sla: "2026-07-29T16:00:00Z"
    estimated_hours: 72    # safety work takes longer; SLA extended
    escalation:
      contact: "safety-lead@example.com"
      trigger: "Root cause traced to model behavior rather than prompt filter"
    cancel_criteria: >
      If regression is in base model behavior, cancel and defer to next model version.
    resolved_at: null
    resolved_by: null
    verification_result: null
Severity-Based Remediation Timelines
Not all blocking concerns require the same remediation timeline. The SLA should be proportional to the severity and complexity of the concern, with an escalation trigger if the SLA cannot be met.
Category
Typical SLA
Extended SLA
Cancel Trigger
Operational (runbook missing, alert not configured)
4h
8h
SLA missed by > 50%
Performance regression (latency, error rate)
Root cause requires architecture change
Test coverage gap
Gap is in a shared library requiring cross-team work
Documentation / comms not ready
24h (hard — no extension)
Never cancel; push launch date instead
Safety / policy compliance regression
72h–1 week
Up to 2 weeks
Base model regression — requires model update
Legal / compliance block
Determined by legal counsel
No extension without legal
Cancel if legal resolution timeline > 30 days

Safety and legal blocks are never time-boxed the same way as operational blocks. The SLA for these is 'as long as it takes to resolve correctly' — the extended SLA above is the maximum before a cancel decision is triggered, not a target.
Tracking Remediation Progress
A remediation plan recorded once and never updated is as useless as no plan at all. Progress must be tracked actively, with status visible to the launch owner and all Go/No-Go participants.
Remediation Status Update Script

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# scripts/update_remediation_status.py
# Updates the status of a remediation plan item in the launch YAML.
import yaml, sys, argparse
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime, timezone

parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument("launch_doc")
parser.add_argument("--id", required=True, help="remediation plan id (e.g. rem-001)")
parser.add_argument("--status", required=True,
                    choices=["open", "in_progress", "resolved", "cancelled"])
parser.add_argument("--resolved-by", help="email of person who resolved/cancelled")
parser.add_argument("--result", help="verification result description")
args = parser.parse_args()

launch_path = Path(args.launch_doc)
launch = yaml.safe_load(launch_path.read_text())
plans = launch.get("remediation_plans", [])

plan = next((p for p in plans if p["id"] == args.id), None)
if not plan:
    print(f"ERROR: remediation plan {args.id} not found in {args.launch_doc}")
    sys.exit(1)

now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
plan["status"] = args.status

if args.status in ("resolved", "cancelled"):
    plan["resolved_at"] = now
    plan["resolved_by"] = args.resolved_by or "unknown"
    plan["verification_result"] = args.result or "(no result provided)"

launch_path.write_text(yaml.dump(launch, default_flow_style=False, sort_keys=False))

# Check if all plans are resolved
all_resolved = all(p.get("status") in ("resolved", "cancelled") for p in plans)
print(f"Updated {args.id} → {args.status}")
if all_resolved:
    print(f"All {len(plans)} remediation plan(s) resolved. Ready to reschedule.")
else:
    remaining = [p['id'] for p in plans if p.get('status') not in ('resolved', 'cancelled')]
    print(f"Remaining open plans: {', '.join(remaining)}")
Remediation Dashboard Report
Communicating the Deferral

A No-Go decision that is not communicated promptly creates confusion: stakeholders who expected a launch at T-0 will not know why it did not happen. The communication must go out within 2 hours of the forum decision.
Deferral Announcement Template
The Reschedule Process

Rescheduling a deferred launch is not the same as starting the launch process over. Most of the pre-launch work is still valid; only the elements affected by the remediation need to be re-verified. The reschedule process makes this explicit.
Reschedule Checklist

# When adding to docs/launches/{service}-{version}.yaml after No-Go:
reschedule:
  original_launch_date: "2026-07-22"
  no_go_date: "2026-07-22"
  new_launch_date: "2026-07-26"
  deferral_reason: "Latency regression and safety compliance regression (rem-001, rem-002)"

  # Items that remain valid from the original launch prep:
  still_valid:
    - "PRR sign-off"
    - "Infrastructure capacity verification"
    - "Support team briefing (update with new date)"
    - "Internal stakeholder comms (resend with new date)"

  # Items that must be re-verified:
  re_verify:
    - item: "Load test (p99 latency)"
      owner: "alice@example.com"
      trigger: "rem-001 fix deployed"

    - item: "Safety eval harness"
      owner: "carol@example.com"
      trigger: "rem-002 fix deployed"

    - item: "Go/No-Go forum"
      owner: "launch-owner"
      trigger: "All re_verify items complete"

    - item: "Update communications with new launch date"
      owner: "launch-owner"
      trigger: "New launch date confirmed"

  # Items that expire and must be fully re-run:
  re_run:
    - item: "Canary traffic test"
      reason: "Canary results are time-bound; stale after 7 days"

    - item: "On-call briefing"
      reason: "On-call rotation may have changed"
Reschedule Automation Script

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# scripts/reschedule_launch.py
# Updates the launch YAML with the new date and generates a reschedule checklist.
import yaml, sys, argparse
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime, timezone

parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument("launch_doc")
parser.add_argument("--new-date", required=True, help="new launch date (YYYY-MM-DD)")
parser.add_argument("--reason", required=True, help="brief reason for deferral")
args = parser.parse_args()

launch_path = Path(args.launch_doc)
launch = yaml.safe_load(launch_path.read_text())

# Validate all remediation plans are resolved before rescheduling
plans = launch.get("remediation_plans", [])
unresolved = [p["id"] for p in plans if p.get("status") not in ("resolved", "cancelled")]
if unresolved:
    print(f"ERROR: Cannot reschedule — unresolved remediation plans: {', '.join(unresolved)}")
    sys.exit(1)

# Record the reschedule
original_date = launch.get("launch_date")
launch["launch_date"] = args.new_date
launch.setdefault("reschedule_history", []).append({
    "original_date": original_date,
    "new_date": args.new_date,
    "reason": args.reason,
    "rescheduled_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
})

# Reset the Go/No-Go decision — must be re-recorded for the new forum
if "go_no_go" in launch:
    launch["go_no_go"]["decision"] = None
    launch["go_no_go"]["decided_at"] = None
    launch["go_no_go"]["decided_by"] = None
    for vote_key in launch["go_no_go"].get("votes", {}):
        launch["go_no_go"]["votes"][vote_key]["vote"] = None

launch_path.write_text(yaml.dump(launch, default_flow_style=False, sort_keys=False))

print(f"Launch rescheduled: {original_date} → {args.new_date}")
print(f"Reason: {args.reason}")
print(f"\nGo/No-Go decision cleared. Run go_nogo_forum.py to record the new forum decision.")
print(f"Re-run: load test, safety eval, canary, on-call briefing before new forum.")
When to Cancel Rather Than Reschedule

Not every deferred launch should be rescheduled. Repeatedly rescheduling a launch that has deep structural problems is worse than cancelling and scoping a smaller, shippable version. The cancel decision should be made explicitly, not reached by default through continued deferral.
Signal
Action
Why
Third consecutive No-Go
Review scope; consider cancelling or splitting the release
Repeated blocks indicate the feature is not launch-ready at the current scope
Remediation requires > 30% of original dev time
Cancel current version; rescope and re-plan
The effort to remediate may exceed the value of the release on the original timeline
Root cause traced to a different team's dependency
Decouple and launch independently; or defer pending the dependency
Blocking your launch on an uncontrolled dependency is not manageable
Safety regression traced to base model behavior
Cancel; defer to next model version
No amount of prompt-level work resolves a model-level safety regression
Business context has changed significantly
Cancel; re-evaluate whether the feature is still needed
A late-stage cancel avoids launching something that no longer serves its purpose
Cancel Process

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# scripts/cancel_launch.py
# Marks a launch as cancelled and generates a cancel notice.
import yaml, sys
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime, timezone

launch_path = Path(sys.argv[1])
reason = sys.argv[2] if len(sys.argv) > 2 else "no reason provided"
launch = yaml.safe_load(launch_path.read_text())

launch["status"] = "cancelled"
launch["cancelled_at"] = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
launch["cancel_reason"] = reason

launch_path.write_text(yaml.dump(launch, default_flow_style=False, sort_keys=False))

print(f"Launch {launch['service']} {launch['version']} cancelled.")
print(f"Reason: {reason}")
print()
print("NEXT STEPS:")
print("1. Notify all Go/No-Go participants of the cancellation.")
print("2. Reverse any staged infrastructure changes (feature flags, DB migrations).")
print("3. Document lessons learned in the retrospective.")
print("4. Archive the launch document to docs/launches/cancelled/.")
print("5. If rescoping: open a new launch document for the smaller scope.")
Post-Remediation Re-check Meeting

After all remediation plans are resolved, the forum reconvenes for a brief re-check — not a full Go/No-Go meeting. The scope is narrower: confirm that the specific concerns raised in the original forum are now satisfied.
Duration: 5–15 minutes, not 30. Only the DRI and the participant(s) who raised the block need to attend.
Agenda: blocker presents their verification result; DRI confirms it meets the pass criteria from the remediation plan; vote is recorded.

If new concerns emerge during re-check that were not present in the original forum, the meeting escalates to a full Go/No-Go. This is uncommon but happens when remediation work itself introduced a regression.
The re-check decision is recorded with the same YAML schema as the original Go/No-Go, tagged with a 'recheck' marker.

# Re-check decision recorded in the same go_no_go block:
go_no_go:
  meeting_time: "2026-07-26T09:00:00Z"
  decision: "GO"
  decided_at: "2026-07-26T09:12:00Z"
  decided_by: "alice@example.com"
  is_recheck: true                    # marks this as a post-remediation recheck
  recheck_scope: ["rem-001", "rem-002"]

  votes:
    engineering:
      participant: "alice@example.com"
      vote: "GO"
      notes: "Load test rerun: p99=187ms at 600 RPS for 30 minutes. rem-001 resolved."

    safety_policy:
      participant: "carol@example.com"
      vote: "GO"
      notes: "Safety eval: 99.95% compliance rate. rem-002 resolved."
Common Failure Patterns in Remediation
Pattern
Symptom
Prevention
Unowned remediation
No owner assigned; concern sits in YAML unresolved
Assign owner before the forum meeting ends; block the Go/No-Go record if owner field is empty
Verbal resolution without verification
Owner marks 'resolved' without re-running the failing check
Verification method and pass criteria are required fields; status only moves to 'resolved' after recorded verification result
SLA slippage without escalation
SLA passes, no escalation triggered
Automated alert when SLA is < 4h away and status is still 'in_progress'
Scope creep during remediation
Fix introduces new features; launch scope expands
Code freeze remains in effect during remediation; only fixes for named concerns are allowed
Third-consecutive No-Go never cancelled
Team keeps rescheduling without ever cancelling
After second reschedule, manager review is required; after third, cancel decision defaults to yes unless explicitly overridden
Re-check escalates to full forum by surprise
Attendees not prepared for a full meeting
Set expectations before the re-check: 'this will become a full forum if new concerns surface'
Summary

No-Go remediation plans transform a blocking decision into a structured path to resolution. The framework here — concern documentation, owner assignment with SLA, objective verification criteria, automated progress tracking, and the reschedule process — ensures that every No-Go either resolves into a successful launch or surfaces the information needed to make an informed cancel decision.
Every block raised in a Go/No-Go forum must have a named owner, a specific remediation action, and objective verification criteria before the meeting ends.
Remediation plans are tracked as structured YAML in the launch document, versioned alongside the code they gate.
SLAs are severity-proportional: 4–8h for operational issues, 24–72h for performance, up to two weeks for safety (with a cancel trigger if unresolved).
After all remediations are resolved, a brief re-check meeting (5–15 minutes) re-verifies only the concerns from the original forum.
After a third consecutive No-Go, the default decision is to cancel the release scope; the DRI must explicitly override this with written justification.
The reschedule process resets the Go/No-Go decision and canary, while leaving still-valid pre-launch work in place.
